ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_00002187 covers congee as a category; no specific chicken congee entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers rice porridge generically; no chicken-specific porridge code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for chicken porridge or congee. None assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of chicken porridge as typically served in a Singapore hawker centre or restaurant, approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Congee / Rice Porridge with Chicken — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA SR Legacy Rice Congee composite
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists chicken porridge (congee) as a Singapore dish. Per-100g values are adapted from HPB Foodo chicken congee entry and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy plain rice congee (NDB 20051) adjusted for typical chicken content (~8% cooked chicken by weight). Values represent a standard Singaporean/Vietnamese-style chicken porridge as served in local restaurants.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for chicken porridge (Chao Ga) found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 78 is based on the closest analogous entry: rice congee / plain rice porridge (GI ~78, glucose reference), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Chicken and aromatics contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (78 × 32.2) / 100 = 25.1, using 350 g serving × 9.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 32.2 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving of chicken porridge provides approximately 980 mg sodium, representing nearly 43% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index from refined white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken porridge has a high GI of approximately 78 and a glycemic load of ~25 per standard serving, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume small portions, pair with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chicken porridge is relatively low in saturated fat (~1.05 g per serving) and cholesterol (~63 mg per serving), making it a heart-friendly option when prepared with lean chicken and minimal added oil, though sodium content should be monitored in patients with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Porridge (Chao Ga) is a comforting rice-based dish made by slow-simmering white rice in chicken broth until the grains break down into a thick, smooth, creamy consistency. The dish is topped with shredded or sliced poached chicken, fresh ginger, spring onions, and often seasoned with fish sauce, white pepper, and a drizzle of sesame oil. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found in Vietnamese restaurants and hawker centres, reflecting the Vietnamese culinary influence on the local food scene. The Vietnamese name 'Cháo Gà' literally means 'chicken congee'. The texture is soft and easily digestible, making it a popular choice for convalescence, light meals, and breakfast. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g bowl provides approximately 217 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 4.2 g fat, and 32.2 g carbohydrate. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. However, its high glycemic index (~78) due to the refined white rice base means it can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation, which is a clinical concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on preparation, primarily from chicken broth and fish sauce, which is relevant for hypertensive patients. The dish is low in saturated fat and is generally considered heart-friendly when prepared with lean chicken.
common_user_queries: Is chicken porridge good for diabetics?
Is chicken porridge high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for 'Chao Ga' as a named dish was found; nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore chicken congee data and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy rice congee, adjusted for typical chicken content. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (plain rice congee) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to adaptation rather than exact match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source entries for this dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries for chicken porridge. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries for this dish. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as chicken porridge prepared from lean chicken and rice contains no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per source data. The dish is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant in Singapore, reflecting Vietnamese culinary heritage adapted to the Singapore food scene. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate based on plain rice congee from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. GL per serving = (78 × 32.2) / 100 = 25.1 for a 350 g bowl.
